coredata.dc:title: Antimicrobial carbon dots/pectin-based hydrogel for promoting healing processes in multidrug-resistant bacteria-infected wounds

coredata.dc:description: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial infections have become a significant threat to global healthcare systems. Here, we developed a highly efficient antimicrobial hydrogel using environmentally friendly garlic carbon dots, pectin, and acrylic acid. The hydrogel had a porous three-dimensional network structure, which endowed it with good mechanical properties and compression recovery performance. The hydrogel could adhere closely to skin tissues and had an equilibrium swelling ratio of 6.21, indicating its potential as a wound dressing. In particular, the bactericidal efficacy following 24-h contact against two MDR bacteria could exceed 99.99 %. When the hydrogel was applied to epidermal wounds infected with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) on mice, a remarkable healing rate of 93.29 % was observed after 10 days. This was better than the effectiveness of the traditionally used antibiotic kanamycin, which resulted in a healing rate of 70.36 %. In vitro cytotoxicity testing and hemolysis assay demonstrated a high biocompatibility. This was further proved by the in vivo assay where no toxic side effects were observed on the heart, liver, spleen, lung, or kidney of mice. This eco-friendly and easy-to-prepare food-inspired hydrogel provides an idea for the rational use of food and food by-products as a wound dressing to control MDR bacterial infections.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design strategy for the food-inspired, adhesive, tough, and antimicrobial GCDs-PAA hydrogel. GCDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. The hydrogel was prepared using GCDs, pectin, AA with APS as an initiator and PEGDA as a cross-linker. The resulting three-dimensional network structure originated from the ester bonds formed between pectin and polyacrylic acid, as well as the hydrogen bonding interactions between the functional groups on the GCDs' surface and the carboxyl groups of pectin and polyacrylic acid.

Morphology and characterization of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. A. Photos and cryo-SEM image of hydrogels; B. Pore size of PAA hydrogel; C. Pore size of GCDs-PAA hydrogel; D. FT-IR spectrum of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels.

Adhesive and mechanical properties of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. A. Visualization of adhesive properties of hydrogels; B. Visualization of compression properties of two hydrogels; C. Compressive stress-strain curve; D. Compressive stress-strain curve (stain range 0–4.5 %); E. Young's modulus; F. Compressive stress-time curve of PAA hydrogel; G. Compressive stress-time curve of GCDs-PAA hydrogel; H. Stress-strain cycle curves of two hydrogels. Histogram bars without common letter differ significantly (P < 0.05).
Swelling properties of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. A. Images of hydrogels after 0, 8, and 16 h of immersion; B. Swelling ratio-time curve of two hydrogels.

Antimicrobial and antibiofilm properties of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. A. Inhibition zones of two hydrogels against MRSA and MRST; B. Bacterial populations of MRSA and MRST after 24 h of contact with two hydrogels; C. Crystal violet staining images of MRSA and MRST biofilms treated with and without hydrogels; D. Eradication percentage of MRSA and MRST biofilms. Histogram bars without common letter differ significantly (P < 0.05).

In vitro release of GCDs from GCDs-PAA hydrogel. A. Effect of pH on the accumulative release of GCDs from GCDs-PAA hydrogel at 12, 24, 48, 72, 96, 120, 144, and 168 h; B. The release kinetics parameters of GCDs from GCDs-PAA hydrogel at different pH values.

In vitro cytotoxicity assay and hemolysis assay of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. A. Cell viability of NIH3T3 cells after co-culture with two hydrogels for 24 h and 48 h; B. Live/dead cell staining of NIH3T3 cells incubated with PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels for 48 h; C. Hemolysis ratio of two hydrogels. Histogram bars without common letter differ significantly (P < 0.05).

In vivo wound healing properties of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. A. Images of wounds in each treatment group at different sampling time points; B. Schematic diagram illustrating the change in wound area size over time for each treatment group; C. Wound healing rate of mice in each treatment group; D. H&E staining of tissues at wound site for each group after 10 days of treatment.
H&E staining of heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney in each treatment group.
Comparisons of the antimicrobial effect of GCDs-PAA hydrogels and other CDs hydrogels against S. aureus.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial infections have become a significant threat to global healthcare systems. Here, we developed a highly efficient antimicrobial hydrogel using environmentally friendly garlic carbon dots, pectin, and acrylic acid. The hydrogel had a porous three-dimensional network structure, which endowed it with good mechanical properties and compression recovery performance. The hydrogel could adhere closely to skin tissues and had an equilibrium swelling ratio of 6.21, indicating its potential as a wound dressing. In particular, the bactericidal efficacy following 24-h contact against two MDR bacteria could exceed 99.99 %. When the hydrogel was applied to epidermal wounds infected with methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) on mice, a remarkable healing rate of 93.29 % was observed after 10 days. This was better than the effectiveness of the traditionally used antibiotic kanamycin, which resulted in a healing rate of 70.36 %. In vitro cytotoxicity testing and hemolysis assay demonstrated a high biocompatibility. This was further proved by the in vivo assay where no toxic side effects were observed on the heart, liver, spleen, lung, or kidney of mice. This eco-friendly and easy-to-prepare food-inspired hydrogel provides an idea for the rational use of food and food by-products as a wound dressing to control MDR bacterial infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin serves as the first defense line of the body against various external threats such as pathogens, environmental pollutions, and physical injuries [1]. Skin tissue is easily infected by pathogenic bacteria after losing its barrier function due to injury [2]. This makes wound healing slow and even causes various complications that threaten the life of patients [3]. Wound infections affect approximately 300 million people globally, costing up to $25 billion for treatment in the United States [4]. In particular, antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections have become a global challenge for public health [4–6]. It's estimated that in 2019, 1.27 million deaths were directly caused by antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections globally, ahead of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and malaria [7]. The emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria has significantly increased the difficulties in treating infections, resulting in more severe cases and a notable rise in mortality rates.

Traditional wound dressings, such as bandages and cotton wool have limitations in removing excess exudates or protecting against bacterial infections [8,9]. Additionally, they need frequent replacement, which can disrupt the healing tissue and impede the overall healing process. Thus, novel wound dressings that can address these limitations and provide a conducive environment for optimal wound healing are urgently needed. In recent years, hydrogel wound dressings have gained increasing popularity because they resemble the three-dimensional structure of biological soft tissues [10–13]. Recent advances in antimicrobial hydrogels have opened up new ways of fighting bacterial infections. This is achieved by incorporating antimicrobial agents such as antibiotics, natural antimicrobials, and nanomaterials into the hydrogel matrix during the fabrication process. For example, Yu et al. [14] developed a chitin hydrogel that could controllably release Ag+ and gentamicin; the hydrogel effectively suppressed the growth of Staphylococcus aureus and promoted the healing of infected wounds in mice. Wang et al. [15] synthesized a bioactive hydrogel that could release antimicrobial peptides in a controlled manner through a visible-light-induced crosslinking strategy. Xiao et al. [16] constructed an Ag/MoS2 nanozyme hydrogel dressing, which inhibited both S. aureus and Escherichia coli by ∼73 % and effectively treated surface skin wounds. Nevertheless, there are concerns over the induction of drug-resistant bacteria and safety of some metal nanoparticles, which have brought new requirements for hydrogel wound dressings.

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing interest because of their ultrasmall size, simple preparation, biocompatibility, water solubility, thermal stability, and optical properties [17–19]. CDs have demonstrated broad-spectrum antimicrobial effect against Gram-negative bacteria, Gram-positive bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites through the induction of oxidative stress, disruption of cell structure, and damage of vital macromolecules (e.g., protein, lipid, and DNA) [20–22]. For example, CDs synthesized using spermidine and o-phenylenediamine showed a low minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 64 μg/mL against E. coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Pseudomonas fluorescens, Aeromonas hydrophila, Yersinia enterocolitica, methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA), and multidrug-resistant Salmonella Typhimurium (MRST) [23]. CDs derived from folic acid at concentrations of 32 and 1000 μg/mL demonstrated a destructive effect on mature biofilms of S. aureus, reducing them by 61 % and 82 %, respectively [24]. N-doped glucose CDs exhibited antifungal activity against Ammophilus fumigatus, Fusarium solani, Penicillium citrinum, Candida albicans, and Rhodotorula rubra with MICs of 19, 78, 19, 27, and 19 μg/mL, respectively [25]. However, chemical-derived CDs have raised safety concerns regarding both the raw materials and photodegradation by-products generated in the process [26,27]. Therefore, it's crucial to explore more environmentally-friendly and cost-effective raw materials to promote the sustainable development of CDs.

In principle, any starting materials containing carbon can be used to prepare CDs. In recent years, green synthesis using food and food waste as raw materials has received significant attention due to its alignment with the concept of sustainability [28,29]. CDs derived from citrus juice inhibited clinical isolates of P. aeruginosa with a MIC of 1.25 % (v/v) [30]. MIC of CDs synthesized from sour whey, a dairy by-product, was 500 and 1000 μg/mL against S. typhimurium and Listeria monocytogenes, respectively [31]. Onion CDs inhibited the growth of Pseudomonas fragi with a MIC of 2000 μg/mL [32]. In terms of antimicrobial properties, CDs derived from food or food by-products are generally not as effective as chemical-derived CDs [33]. As synthesis processes usually retain some properties of the precursor materials, we synthesized CDs using garlic, a natural antimicrobial plant, to explore food-derived CDs with promising antimicrobial efficacy [34]. As low as 25 μg/mL of the garlic CDs (GCDs) effectively inhibited the growth of MRSA, which is a common cause of bacterial infections [33]. Despite the promising antimicrobial effects and high biocompatibility of GCDs, there is a lack of information on using them in hydrogel wound dressing.

In this study, GCDs, acrylic acid (AA), and pectin were used as raw materials, and ammonium persulfate (APS) was used as initiator. Polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate (PEGDA) was used as a cross-linking agent to construct a composite antibacterial hydrogel system. The adhesive properties, mechanical properties, swelling properties, antibacterial properties, and biocompatibility of the hydrogel were evaluated. In addition, the antimicrobial hydrogel was tested in a mouse model of MRSA-infected epidermal wounds to assess its effectiveness in promoting wound healing.

Garlic was obtained from the Wuxi Agricultural Product Market in Jiangsu Province. AA (≥98 %), APS (≥98 %), anhydrous ethanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Luria-Bertani (LB) broth, Trypticase Soy broth (TSB), and LB agar were purchased from National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Shanghai Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pectin (galacturonic acid ≥74.0 %) and PEGDA (containing stabilizer methoxyhydroquinone, average M
n = 736) were purchased from Shanghai Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The molecular weight (Mw) of pectin was analyzed using a high-performance gel filtration chromatography (HPGFC) system. The analysis was performed on a Waters 1525 HPLC system, which included a 2410 differential refractive index detector and an Empower workstation. Pectin was dissolved in 20 mM potassium dihydrogen phosphate at 5 mg/mL, passed through a 0.45 μm filter, and injected into an Ultrahydrogel™ Linear HPLC column (300 mm × 7.8 mm id). The column was maintained at 40 °C, and eluted by 0.1 M NaAc at a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min. The standard curve was plotted using a range of dextran T-2000 (Mw 200 kDa). Kanamycin sulfate was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CCK-8 and trypsin cell digestion solution (containing 0.25 % EDTA) were purchased from Shanghai Biyuntian Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) were purchased from Gibco Invitrogen Corporation (CA, USA). MRSA and MRST were stored in our lab [33]. Mouse embryonic fibroblasts 3T3 (NIH3T3) was purchased from the cell bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

GCDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method following our previous publication [33]. Briefly, garlic cloves were 1:4 (w/v) mixed with ultra-pure water and blended into garlic juice, which was filtered and heated at 210 °C for 4 h to synthesize GCDs. GCDs were centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 20 min, passed through a 0.22 μm filter, dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag with ultra-pure water for 6 h, freeze-dried under vacuum, and stored at 4 °C before use.

To prepare GCDs-pectin-acrylic acid (GCDs-PAA) hydrogel, 10 mL of the GCD solution at 1.6 mg/mL, 15 mL of NaOH at 2.5 mmol/L, 5 mL of acrylic acid, 150 μL of PEGDA, and 0.4 g of pectin were mixed to prepare a homogeneous solution. Then, 0.4 g of APS was added as an initiator, and the mixture was heated at 50 °C for 20 min. The resulting solution was transferred to a mold and placed in a drying oven at 60 °C for 30 min to form the GCD-PAA hydrogel. Control PAA hydrogel was prepared by replacing the GCD solution with 10 mL of 2.5 mmol/L NaOH in the above formula.

The hydrogels were quickly frozen in liquid nitrogen, transferred to a vacuum sample chamber, and sublimated at −90 °C for 10 min. The resulting samples were fractured to expose the internal section, which was then fixed on the stage by conductive glue and sprayed with gold. The internal microstructure of the hydrogels was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 450, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The hydrogels were also freeze-dried for two days and characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) from 600 to 4000 cm−1.

Hydrogel samples measuring 15 mm in diameter and 10 mm in thickness were prepared to test the mechanical properties. The adhesion properties of hydrogels on the skin were tested and visually recorded by a camera. Compression properties were evaluated using an Instron 5969 electronic universal testing machine (MA, USA) equipped with a 250 N sensor. The compression test was conducted at a speed of 10 mm/min until the hydrogels reached their maximum deformation limit. The compression recovery performance of hydrogels was assessed through a compression cycle test. A 250 N sensor was used with a compression speed of 20 mm/min. The deformation limit was set at 60 %, and the compression was repeated for 10 cycles.

The swelling properties of PAA hydrogel and GCDs-PAA hydrogel were characterized by testing their swelling ratios in PBS until they reached constant swelling [35]. Firstly, the initial mass of the hydrogels was measured and recorded as M0. The hydrogel samples were then placed in PBS and held at 37 °C. The hydrogel samples were removed with tweezers hourly, blotted dry, weighed, and recorded as Mt. The swelling ratio of the hydrogel sample was calculated with the following equation:
Swelling ratio =
Mt − M 0
M 0
× 100 %

MRSA and MRST strains were inoculated into LB broth and incubated overnight at 37 °C under shaking at 220 rpm. After reaching an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of 1.0, the bacterial suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The bacterial pellets obtained were washed three times with sterile PBS and subsequently resuspended in PBS prior to use.

Hydrogel samples were first immersed in ultra-pure water for 15 min, then in 75 % ethanol for 60 min to sterilize hydrogels and remove excessive monomers, initiators, or cross-linkers. The sterilized hydrogels were rinsed three times with sterile PBS to remove ethanol residual and transferred to a sterile petri dish before use.

To evaluate the antimicrobial effect of hydrogels, 100 μL of the prepared bacterial suspension diluted to 1 × 105 CFU/mL was evenly spread onto each LB agar plate. Hydrogel samples with a diameter of 15 mm and height of 5 mm were placed at the center of LB plates and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h to measure the diameters of inhibition zones. In addition, 30 μL of bacterial suspension at 1 × 108 CFU/mL was pipetted onto the surface of each hydrogel and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Populations of viable Cbacteria were enumerated by adding 1 mL sterile PBS, serially diluting, and spread plating onto LB agar plates.

Overnight cultures of MRSA and MRST were diluted in TSB (1 × 108 CFU/mL) and added to a 24-well plate, which was then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to form biofilms and washed with PBS. Either sterilized PAA or GCDs-PAA hydrogels (15 mm diameter, 4 mm height) with 500 μL of sterile distilled water were added to each treatment well. Control wells were treated with 500 μL of distilled water. Each treatment had three wells. The plate was further incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Biofilm formed at the bottom was first fixed with 200 μL of 100 % methanol per well for 15 min and then quantified using crystal violet assay [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release of GCDs from GCDs-PAA hydrogels at different pH
ce:section.ce:para:
GCDs release study was conducted according to a published method [37]. Briefly, GCDs-PAA hydrogels were added to 10 mL of PBS (pH = 3.0, 7.4, and 9.0) and incubated at 37 °C under shaking at 100 rpm. The release solution was sampled at 0, 12, 24, 48, 72, 96, 120, 144, and 168 h with three replicates per treatment. At each sampling time, 1 mL of PBS was withdrawn to measure fluorescence and added back to maintain the original volume. The release solution was analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm and emission wavelength of 442 nm. The release of GCDs was determined by calculating the cumulative release using a standard curve. The first-order kinetics model, Higuchi model, and Korsmeyer-Peppas model were used to study the release kinetics of GCDs from GCDs-PAA hydrogels [38].

where Mt is the cumulative release at time t, M∞ is the cumulative release at time ∞, k is the model constant, and n is the release exponent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and hemolytic assays
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity and hemolytic testing were performed following our published method [33]. Briefly, NIH3T3 cells were cultured in DMEM with and without hydrogels in 96-well plates. A blank group with DEME but without cells or hydrogels was used as a negative control. After 24 h and 48 h of incubation at 37 °C and 5 % CO2, cell viability was determined using a CCK-8 kit with six replicates per treatment. Cell viability was further evaluated by live/dead staining. After 48 h of incubation, NIH3T3 cells were gently washed with PBS, stained with calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) per manufacturer's instructions, and observed under a fluorescence microscope.

In the hemolysis assay, red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by centrifuging fresh human blood at 1200 rpm for 5 min and washed with sterile saline for three times. Then, 50 μL of RBCs were added to 950 μL of saline along with one piece of hydrogel (5 mm diameter, 3 mm thickness). The same amounts of RBCs were also added to saline as a negative control and deionized (DI) water as a positive control. All treatment groups were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h before centrifuging at 1000 rpm for 15 min. Hemolysis ratios were determined by measuring OD540 values of the supernatants and calculated using the following formula:
Hemolysis
%
=
OD 540 − hydrogels − OD 540 − saline
OD 540 − DI
water − OD 540 − saline
× 100 % .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact on wound healing on the backs of mice and in vivo toxicity
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the antimicrobial effect of GCDs hydrogels in vivo, epidermal wound bacterial infection model was established using female BALB/c mice (6–8 weeks of age, 18–22 g) according to the published method [39]. The mice were anesthetized with pentobarbital sodium at a dose of 70 mg/kg and fixed on the operating table. Local disinfection was performed using an iodine solution, and the fur on the back of the mice was shaved. A 5-mm diameter incision was made on the back of each mouse, and 15 μL of MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was inoculated into the wound. The mice were then randomly divided into four experimental groups with three mice in each group. The wounds of mice in the four experimental groups were treated with the following four samples: (1) 50 μL saline; (2) 50 μL kanamycin sulfate at 2 mg/mL; (3) PAA hydrogel (15 mm diameter, 4 mm height); (4) GCDs-PAA hydrogel (15 mm diameter, 4 mm height). The wounds were photographed every 24 h, and the infected skin area was calculated with Image J. The wound healing rate on day X was calculated as [40]:
Wound healing rate
%
=
Area
on
Day
0 − Area
on

X

× 100 %

After ten days of treatment, all mice were sacrificed, and wound tissues, heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney samples were collected. The samples were fixed with 10 % formalin for wound tissues and 4 % paraformaldehyde for all other organs, embedded in paraffin blocks, sectioned, and stained by hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) for histopathological analysis.

All animal procedures were conducted in accordance with the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals [41]. The protocols were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Jiangnan University.

Each experiment was performed in triplicate and repeated independently at least twice. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Duncan's test was used to analyze the differences between the data sets. Differences in the mean values were considered significant at P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design rationale and structure of antibacterial hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The Mw of pectin was 101.39 kDa (Fig. S1). The GCDs-PAA hydrogel was prepared in two steps (Fig. 1). Firstly, nitrogen and sulfur self-doped GCDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. According to our previous publication of GCDs [33], their particle size varied between 1.5 and 4.5 nm, averaging 3.1 ± 0.23 nm. GCDs were positively charged with a zeta potential of +8.53 ± 0.41 eV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed that GCDs had atomic percentages of 73.62 % carbon, 16.32 % oxygen, 9.62 % nitrogen, and 0.44 % sulfur. The surface of GCDs was rich in amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Secondly, GCDs, pectin, and AA monomers were mixed to prepare the GCDs-PAA hydrogels. APS was used as an initiator for the radical polymerization of AA, which then formed an interpenetrating network with pectin via ester bonds [42]. GCDs interacted with the PAA network through hydrogen bonds.

The Mw of pectin was 101.39 kDa (Fig. S1). The GCDs-PAA hydrogel was prepared in two steps (Fig. 1). Firstly, nitrogen and sulfur self-doped GCDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. According to our previous publication of GCDs [33], their particle size varied between 1.5 and 4.5 nm, averaging 3.1 ± 0.23 nm. GCDs were positively charged with a zeta potential of +8.53 ± 0.41 eV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed that GCDs had atomic percentages of 73.62 % carbon, 16.32 % oxygen, 9.62 % nitrogen, and 0.44 % sulfur. The surface of GCDs was rich in amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Secondly, GCDs, pectin, and AA monomers were mixed to prepare the GCDs-PAA hydrogels. APS was used as an initiator for the radical polymerization of AA, which then formed an interpenetrating network with pectin via ester bonds [42]. GCDs interacted with the PAA network through hydrogen bonds.

SEM was used to characterize the internal microstructure of hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 2A, both PAA hydrogels and GCDs-PAA hydrogels had continuous porous structures. The pore size of the hydrogels was calculated based on the electron microscopy images (Fig. 2B&C). The average pore sizes of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels were 1.08 ± 0.51 and 1.00 ± 0.49 μm, respectively. GCDs-PAA hydrogels showed a slightly denser pore structure with more numbers of small pores (size ≤0.5 μm). This is possibly due to the additional hydrogen bonding between GCDs and the carboxyl groups of pectin and polyacrylic acid [43]. Small-sized pores can contribute to the mechanical stability of hydrogels [44].

FT-IR was used to analyze the functional groups in the hydrogels (Fig. 2D). The peaks at 2930 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of CH, which is common in hydrogels [33]. The absorption peaks at 1698 cm−1 (C=O) show that ester bonds were formed in both hydrogels [33]. This suggests that pectin and AA were successfully crosslinked with PEGDA. The peak intensity of the GCDs-PAA hydrogel was stronger than that of the PAA hydrogel, indicating a higher ester group content of the GCDs-PAA hydrogel [45]. The bands at 1447 and 1232 cm−1 correspond to the O-C=O and C-O-C asymmetry stretching vibrations of -COOCH3, respectively. The absorption peaks between 1160 and 1013 cm−1 were due to the CO stretching vibration of the C-O/C-C and C-OH of the ring in pectin, respectively [45].

The adhesion property of PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels was firstly tested on human skin (Fig. 3A). The two hydrogels could tightly adhere to the skin tissue, whether upright or inverted, without coming loose or falling off. This characteristic is desirable for wound dressing applications.

Fig. 3B shows the state of the two hydrogels during compression. Both of them maintained a complete form without breakage or fracture under compression. Instron 5969 universal electronic testing machine was further used to quantify the compression properties of the two hydrogels. When the strain was <60 %, the stress of both PAA and GCDs-PAA hydrogels increased slowly with the increase of the strain (Fig. 3C). When the strain was >60 %, the stress of the two hydrogels increased rapidly, and the growth rate of the stress of the GCDs-PAA hydrogels was slightly higher than that of the PAA hydrogels. Under the same stress, the change in the strain of the GCDs-PAA hydrogels was smaller. This indicates that the deformation resistance of the GCDs-PAA hydrogels was stronger. The final maximum stress and strain for PAA hydrogel were 91.02 % and 271.6 kPa, respectively. For GCDs-PAA hydrogel, the values were measured as 88.68 % and 282.3 kPa, respectively.

When the strain was in the range of 0–4.5 %, the stress and strain basically showed a linear relationship (Fig. 3D). The slope of the trend line in this region is the Young's modulus of hydrogel, reflecting the compression resistance and rebound speed of hydrogel. The Young's modulus of CDS-PAA hydrogel was 5.59 kPa, which was more than twice higher than that of PAA hydrogel (2.51 kPa, Fig. 3E). Young's modulus is associated with the crosslinking density of hydrogels [46]. The enhanced compression resistance of the hydrogels after the addition of GCDs may be attributed to the hydrogen bonding force between the amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of GCDs and the carboxyl group of pectin and polyacrylic acid, as well as the van der Waals force between adjacent GCDs in the hydrogel system, which enhances the rigidity of the GCDs-PAA hydrogels [47].

Then the resilience of the two hydrogels was quantitatively tested by cyclic compression experiment. As shown in Fig. 3F and G, both PAA hydrogels and GCDs-PAA hydrogels could maintain their original compression characteristics after at least ten compression cycles within the strain range of 0–60 %, and the stress variation trend between cycles did not change significantly. The stress-strain recovery curves of the two hydrogels are shown in Fig. 3H. With the increase in compression times, the recovery curves of the two hydrogels showed no significant changes compared with the previous cycle. The above results indicate that the two hydrogels had good compressive fatigue resistance [48]. The recovery curve of GCDs-PAA hydrogels was narrower than that of PAA hydrogels, indicating that GCDs-PAA hydrogels had better recovery performance. In the GCDs-PAA hydrogel system, the electrostatic repulsion force and hydrogen bond force between GCDs made them evenly dispersed in the entire gel network. This is conducive to the formation of physical cross-linking between GCDs and pectin chains, and this physical crosslinking gives better recovery performance to GCDs-PAA hydrogels [23].

Following the bacterial infection, wounds on the skin surface often release tissue fluid or blood as a result of inflammation. The presence of excess exudate in the wound hinders the healing process. An ideal wound dressing should effectively retain wound exudate without forming effusion [49]. The swelling and hygroscopic properties of PAA hydrogels and GCDs-PAA hydrogels were evaluated by recording the mass changes of PAA hydrogels and GCDs-PAA hydrogels in the PBS solution over time at 37 °C. PBS was used to simulate the tissue fluid exudated from the wound site. As shown in Fig. 4A, after soaking in the PBS solution for 8 h, both hydrogels absorbed water and expanded, and their volumes increased significantly. When the soaking time was increased to 16 h, the volume of the two hydrogels continued to increase. The swelling ratio was further calculated by the mass of hydrogel samples. The mass of PAA hydrogel and GCDs-PAA hydrogel increased rapidly with the extension of time from 0 to 16 h, and this trend gradually slowed down after 16 h (Fig. 4B). PAA hydrogel and GCDs-PAA hydrogel reached swelling equilibrium at 28 and 32 h, with equilibrium swelling ratios of 7.12 and 6.21, respectively. After the addition of GCDs, the equilibrium swelling ratio of the hydrogel decreased, which may be attributed to the smaller pores of the GCDs-PAA hydrogel network than those of the PAA hydrogel. Therefore, GCDs-PAA hydrogels could absorb excess tissue fluid at the wound site without excessive expansion, thus improving their adhesion stability as dressings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and antibiofilm properties of hydrogels in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Two MDR bacteria MRSA and MRST were selected to evaluate the antibacterial ability of PAA hydrogels and GCDs-PAA hydrogels. In the inhibition zone assay (Fig. 5A), the PAA hydrogel did not exhibit significant inhibition against MRSA or MRST, while GCDs-PAA hydrogel showed a distinct inhibitory zone for both bacteria. The width of inhibitory zones for MRSA and MRST was 10.1 and 6.2 mm, respectively. The antimicrobial activity of the two hydrogels was further quantitatively analyzed by the surface contact assay. After 24 h of contact, there were no significant differences in viable bacterial populations between the control and the PAA hydrogel group (Fig. 5B). However, the number of MRST and MRSA in the GCDs-PAA hydrogel group decreased by 3.45 Log CFU/mL and 4.59 Log CFU/mL, respectively. In particular, after 24 h of exposure, the bactericidal rate against MRSA, a prominent source of skin and soft tissue infections, exceeded 99.99 %.

It is worth noting that the bactericidal effect of GCDs-PAA hydrogel was generally superior to hydrogels prepared with chemical-derived CDs (Table 1 ). The promising inhibitory effect is likely attributed to the release of GCDs [23,42,50]. GCDs have been proven to kill bacteria through penetration into bacteria via electrostatic interaction, cellular structure disruption, and induction of bacterial oxidative stress response [33]. The superior antimicrobial effect of GCDs-PAA hydrogels against MRSA than that of MRST was consistent with the antibacterial performance of GCDs. It is possibly due to the differences in bacterial structures, which affect the cell-surface interaction and subsequent internationalization of GCDs [51,52].

Biofilm is defined as microbial communities embedded in an extracellular polymeric matrix (EPS) [58]. In chronic wounds, bacteria primarily exist in biofilm form rather than planktonic form; the presence of this biofilm leads to increased inflammation and slows down the healing process of wounds [36]. Therefore, the biofilm eradication effect of hydrogels was also evaluated on MDR bacteria (Fig. 5C&D). PAA hydrogel alone was ineffective in removing biofilms from either bacterium. In contrast, GCDs-PAA hydrogel significantly reduced MRSA and MRST biofilms by 62 % and 45 %, respectively (Fig. 5D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release of GCDs from GCDs-PAA hydrogels at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial and antibiofilm activities of GCDs-PAA hydrogels were likely due to the release of antimicrobial GCDs (Fig. 6A). At a neutral pH of 7.4, an initial burst release of GCDs was observed within the first 24 h, reaching 21.1 ± 6.3 %. From there, the release gradually increased to 34.5 ± 3.8 % by day 4 and maintained a similar level until day 7. At pH levels of 3.0 and 9.0, the release of GCDs was slightly faster, but not significantly different from the release at pH 7.4. After 7 days of incubation, GCDs release at pH 3.0 and 9.0 were 39.2 ± 3.2 % and 41.2 ± 3.4 %, respectively.

The release curves at different pH were fitted using first-order kinetics, Higuchi, and Korsmeyer-Peppas models to explore the release mechanism (Fig. 6B). Adjusted R2 was used to determine the best-fit model. The R2 values of the first-order kinetic release model ranged from 0.986 to 0.994 at different pH levels, which were higher compared to Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models, with respective values of 0.911–0.924 and 0.971–0.979. Thus, the first-order kinetics model was the best-fitting model for GCDs-PAA hydrogel, indicating that GCDs were mainly absorbed into the inner wall of the pores in the hydrogel [59]. The diffusion constant (n) in the Korsmeyer-Peppas model determines the type of release mechanisms. When n is smaller than 0.45, the release follows the Fickian diffusion mechanism [38]. In this study, the n values for GCDs-PAA hydrogels were 0.29–0.32 regardless of the pH of the release solution. This indicates that Fickian diffusion was the predominant mechanism. As the swelling of hydrogels reached equilibrium faster than the GCDs release rate, a significant portion of the diffusion occurred within the already swollen hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and hemolytic evaluation of antibacterial hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, after a skin injury in mammals, fibroblasts help to heal wounds by producing collagen and the extracellular matrix [60]. Therefore, NIH3T3 cells were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of hydrogels. After 24 h of co-incubation, NIH3T3 cell activity of PAA hydrogel and GCDs-PAA hydrogel groups was not significantly different from that of the control group (Fig. 7A). Even after extending the co-incubation time to 48 h, the cell viability of the two experimental groups remained above 90 % and there was no significant difference from the control group. Cell viability was further confirmed by the live/dead staining results (Fig. 7B). The above results indicate that PAA hydrogel itself would not affect the normal growth of NIH3T3 cells, and the hydrogel system could still maintain good cytocompatibility after the addition of GCDs.

The biocompatibility of antibacterial hydrogels was further evaluated by the hemolysis test. The results of the hemolysis test are shown in Fig. 7C. In the positive control group (DI water), no deposition of RBCs was observed at the bottom of the centrifuge tube. The solution appeared red and clear, indicating that severe hemolysis occurred in RBCs after co-incubation with deionized water. This hemolysis rate was defined as 100 %. No hemolysis occurred in the negative control group added with saline. The supernatant in the centrifuge tube was colorless and transparent, and the hemolysis rate was defined as 0 %. The hemolysis rate of the PAA hydrogel was not significantly different from that of saline. The supernatant of the GCDs-PAA hydrogel group showed an extremely slight red color, and the hemolysis rate was determined to be 2.41 % after quantitative analysis with the enzyme label instrument. This met the blood safety requirements of the international standards for clinical use of drugs [61,62]. The results of the hemolysis experiment once again verified the high biocompatibility of GCDs-PAA hydrogel, which provided a safe and reliable basis for its application in the antibacterial infection field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound healing effect of hydrogels in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of PAA hydrogels and GCDs-PAA hydrogels to promote MDR bacterial wound healing was further evaluated by establishing an MRSA-infected wound model on the back of mice. Fig. 8A shows the wound healing process of mice in each treatment group. The wound area was further visualized on a 10 mm × 10 mm coordinate system and quantitatively calculated using Image J (Fig. 8B). The results showed that on the third day after treatment, the wounds of mice in the four treatment groups were healed to a certain extent. Among them, the wound healing rate of the GCDs-PAA hydrogel group was the highest at 38.55 % (Fig. 8B&C). On the fifth day after treatment, the wound healing rates of mice in the control group, kanamycin sulfate group, PAA hydrogel group, and GCDs-PAA hydrogel group were 22.7 %, 28.44 %, 30.99 % and 55.09 %, respectively. The wound healing rates in the GCDs-PAA hydrogel group were significantly higher than those in other groups. On the seventh day after treatment, the wound area of mice in each group was further reduced, the wound healing rate of mice in the GCDs-PAA hydrogel group was up to 85.35 %, the abscess at the wound site was significantly reduced, and epidermal regeneration was observed. On the tenth day after treatment, there were still large wounds and abscesses in the control group, and the healing rate was only 51.24 %. The wound healing rate of the PAA hydrogel group was 57.03 %, which was not significantly different from that of the control group. The wound surface of mice in the kanamycin sulfate group shrank significantly compared with day 0, and the healing rate was 70.36 %. The therapeutic effect of GCDs-PAA hydrogel on the wound of mice was significantly better than that of the other three groups. The wound of mice was basically closed, and the healing rate was as high as 93.29 %. Through the analysis of the healing process of mice, it was seen that GCDs-PAA hydrogel could promote the skin wound healing of mice infected with MRSA, and the therapeutic effect was better than that of kanamycin sulfate, a traditional aminoglycoside antibiotic. As PAA hydrogel alone did not possess wound healing properties, it can be inferred that incorporating GCDs could confer the antibacterial and antibiofilm abilities. This enabled GCDs-PAA hydrogel to inhibit bacterial reproduction and reduce inflammation at the wound site. In addition, the excellent mechanical properties of GCDs-PAA hydrogel enabled it to better fit the wound tissue and promote the release of GCDs. The good swelling and hygroscopic properties help remove excess exudate in the wound area, which is beneficial for wound healing.

H&E staining was performed on the wound site for histopathological analysis to further evaluate the wound recovery after ten days of treatment. As shown in Fig. 8D, the epidermis of mice in the control group was severely damaged. The wound displayed disorganized tissue structure, and there was a notable accumulation of inflammatory cells. In the kanamycin sulfate group and PAA hydrogel group, mice showed evident scab tissue formation at the wound site. The kanamycin sulfate group exhibited a decrease in inflammatory cells and a limited amount of fibroblast proliferation, resulting in a higher degree of tissue healing. Mice in the GCDs-PAA hydrogel group displayed improved integrity of the wound epithelium and connective tissue. There were minimal inflammatory cells observed around the wound, which can be attributed to the bacteriostatic effect of GCDs against MRSA. In addition, more fibroblasts and hair follicles were observed in the wound area, accompanied by noticeable hyperplasia in the spinous layer and basal layers. These results indicate that GCDs-PAA hydrogel could effectively combat the epidermal wound infection caused by MRSA in mice, inhibit inflammation, and promote wound healing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity evaluation of antibacterial hydrogels in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Biosafety is a crucial factor for clinical anti-infection applications. Therefore, H&E staining was performed on key organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of mice after treatment to evaluate the impact of different materials (Fig. 9 ). No accumulation of inflammatory cells was found in the heart, liver, spleen, lung or kidney tissues of mice in the control group, kanamycin sulfate group, PAA hydrogel group and CDS-PAA hydrogel group. Morphology of cells and tissues was normal without obvious organ injury. This indicates that MRSA infection of surface wounds did not cause organ inflammation in mice, and the prepared PAA hydrogels and CDS-PAA hydrogels both had good biocompatibility in vivo.

We synthesized a GCDs-PAA hydrogel using GCDs, AA, and pectin with high adhesiveness, toughness, antimicrobial properties, and biocompatibility. The most significant advantage of the hydrogel was its promising antimicrobial efficacy. The bactericidal rate of 24-h contact against MRSA exceeded 99.99 %. The healing rate of MRSA-infected mouse epidermal wounds was 93.29 % after ten days of treatment with GCDs-PAA hydrogel, and the therapeutic effect was better than the traditional antibiotic kanamycin sulfate. On the one hand, GCDs endowed GCDs-PAA hydrogels with good antibacterial ability; on the other hand, the excellent mechanical properties of GCDs-PAA hydrogels enabled them to better fit the wound tissue and promote the release of GCDs. Their good swelling and high absorbency also facilitated the removal of excess exudate and promoted the wound healing without inducing toxic side effects. In summary, this environmentally friendly antimicrobial hydrogel offers a practical approach for utilizing food and food by-products to fight against MDR bacterial infections.
